local_names.romanized_local: Rou gui (Chinese); Kayu manis (Malay); Ilavaṅkappattai (Tamil); Dālacīnī (Hindi)

cuisine: Global spice; widely used in Singaporean, South Asian, Middle Eastern, and Western cuisines
region_of_origin: Sri Lanka (Ceylon cinnamon); also China and Southeast Asia (Cassia cinnamon)

main_ingredients: cinnamon bark (Cinnamomum verum or Cinnamomum cassia)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical culinary serving of ground cinnamon is approximately 1 teaspoon (about 2.6 g), used as a spice or flavouring in cooking, baking, or beverages.

nutrient_source.title: Cinnamon, ground — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID: 02010)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate) cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo database (data_source: hpb, confidence: 0.95). HPB values used for energy (329 kcal), protein (3.3 g), fat (1.0 g), carbohydrate (76.6 g), calcium (17 mg), iron (22.2 mg). Secondary nutrients (dietary fibre, sodium, saturated fat, sugar, potassium) sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for ground cinnamon.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Ground cinnamon has a very low GI estimated at approximately 5 due to its extremely high dietary fibre content (53.1 g/100g) and negligible available carbohydrate at culinary doses. At a typical serving of 2.6 g, the glycemic load is effectively negligible (GL ≈ 0.1). GI of 5 is consistent with published estimates for high-fibre spices in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). Cinnamon is also documented to reduce postprandial glycaemia via cinnamaldehyde-mediated insulin sensitisation.

hypertension.key_factor: Negligible sodium content (~10 mg/100g); high potassium (431 mg/100g); used in very small culinary doses
hypertension.guidance: Ground cinnamon is safe for individuals with hypertension; at typical culinary doses of 1–5 g per serving, sodium contribution is less than 1 mg and potassium content may support vascular health — however, blood pressure management should rely on overall dietary sodium restriction and medical therapy, not spice supplementation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Cinnamaldehyde improves insulin sensitivity; very high dietary fibre (53.1 g/100g); negligible glycemic load at culinary doses (GL ≈ 0.1 per teaspoon)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ground cinnamon is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; cinnamaldehyde has evidence-based hypoglycaemic properties by improving insulin receptor sensitivity and reducing postprandial glucose, and at culinary doses (1–5 g/day) the glycemic load is negligible — however, cassia cinnamon at doses above 6 g/day carries coumarin-related hepatotoxicity risk, and cinnamon must not replace prescribed antidiabetic medication.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.3 g/100g); negligible cholesterol; anti-inflammatory cinnamaldehyde; high dietary fibre
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Ground cinnamon poses no cardiovascular risk at culinary doses; its saturated fat and cholesterol content are negligible, its high dietary fibre may support LDL reduction, and cinnamaldehyde has demonstrated anti-inflammatory and lipid-modulating properties in clinical studies — individuals with cardiovascular disease may safely include cinnamon as a flavouring spice within a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Cinnamaldehyde inhibits NF-κB, reduces IL-6 and TNF-alpha; negligible saturated fat (0.3 g/100g)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Ground cinnamon is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; cinnamaldehyde is an evidence-based anti-inflammatory compound that inhibits NF-κB signalling and reduces pro-inflammatory cytokines IL-6 and TNF-alpha — culinary use of 0.5–2 g/day is safe and may provide modest anti-inflammatory benefit, with Ceylon cinnamon preferred over cassia to avoid coumarin-related hepatotoxicity with regular use.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ground cinnamon is a fine reddish-brown aromatic powder derived from the dried inner bark of Cinnamomum trees, primarily Cinnamomum verum (Ceylon or true cinnamon, from Sri Lanka) and Cinnamomum cassia (cassia cinnamon, from China and Southeast Asia). The bark is harvested, dried, and ground into a powder with a warm, sweet, slightly spicy flavour and a distinctive fragrant aroma driven by the volatile compound cinnamaldehyde. Ground cinnamon is used globally as a spice in both sweet and savoury applications — in Singaporean and South Asian cooking it flavours biryanis, curries, and desserts; in Middle Eastern cuisine it seasons meat dishes and rice; in Western baking it is used in pastries, cakes, and hot beverages. Nutritionally, per 100 g, ground cinnamon provides 329 kcal, 3.3 g protein, 1.0 g fat (of which only 0.3 g is saturated), and 76.6 g carbohydrate — however, 53.1 g of that carbohydrate is dietary fibre, making the available (glycaemic) carbohydrate very low. At a typical culinary serving of 1 teaspoon (approximately 2.6 g), the glycemic load is negligible (GL ≈ 0.1). Cinnamon is exceptionally rich in iron (22.2 mg/100g) and potassium (431 mg/100g). Clinically, cinnamaldehyde has documented hypoglycaemic properties — it improves insulin receptor sensitivity and reduces postprandial glucose response — making cinnamon a beneficial spice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Its anti-inflammatory properties (NF-κB inhibition, reduction of IL-6 and TNF-alpha) are relevant for rheumatoid arthritis management. Sodium content is negligible (~10 mg/100g), making it safe for hypertension. Cassia cinnamon contains coumarin, which at doses above 6 g/day may cause hepatotoxicity; Ceylon cinnamon is preferred for regular supplemental use. Cinnamon is vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher.
common_user_queries: How much cinnamon is safe per day?
What is the difference between Ceylon and cassia cinnamon?
How many calories in a teaspoon of cinnamon?
Is cinnamon safe for high blood pressure?
What is the glycemic index of cinnamon?
What is cinnamon called in Chinese / Malay / Tamil / Hindi?

GI of 5 is an analogous estimate consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008, Diabetes Care) for high-fibre spices; at a 2.6 g serving, GL is negligible at 0.1.
